topic: A person discovers the tree they planted as a child has been cut down
raw_output:
[story 1]

The splintered stumps were the first thing that hit me. I stood at the edge of the clearing, the familiar, towering silhouette of the oak I’d nurtured since I was seven now reduced to jagged, pale remnants. My hands clenched into fists so tight my knuckles shone white. It hadn't been an accident; I’d seen the heavy machinery tracks leading right up to the perimeter last week. A cold, hard knot tightened in my chest, and I took a slow, deliberate breath, the air tasting metallic. "Well," I muttered, the sound rough in my throat, "that's a bold move." I didn't move to call anyone. I just stared at the empty space where my childhood sanctuary used to stand, a stubborn set to my jaw refusing to crumble under the shock.

[story 2]

I walked the line of broken wood, my boots crunching on the debris. Each fallen branch felt like a small, sharp accusation. When I finally reached the spot, the silence was deafening, broken only by the distant hum of traffic. I knelt down, running my fingers over the raw, exposed roots—the very roots I had carefully tended in my youth. A surge of hot anger flooded my system, making my vision narrow. I stood up abruptly, brushing the dirt from my jeans with aggressive force. "You think you can just erase that?" I spat the words out, my voice surprisingly steady despite the tremor running through my limbs. I turned my back on the devastation, walking away with a determined stride, refusing to let my shoulders slump.

[story 3]

The sight of the cleared earth made my stomach churn. It was the ghost of my backyard, the place where I used to hide and draw maps of imaginary kingdoms. I watched the heavy equipment parked nearby, the indifferent metal gleaming under the afternoon sun. A slow, simmering heat rose in my neck. I didn't cry; tears felt too weak for the magnitude of the disrespect. I walked toward the largest stump, placing my palm flat against the rough bark. I didn't shout. Instead, I leaned in close, my gaze fixed on the ruined wood, and whispered, low and intensely, "You'll have to learn something about ownership." Then, I straightened up, turned sharply on my heel, and walked toward the road, my pace unwavering, every step a declaration.
